question_title: Will any single oil spill larger than 50,000 barrels occur in the Gulf of Oman before May 1, 2026?
summary: Despite a severe ongoing maritime conflict in the Strait of Hormuz/Gulf of Oman region since February 28, 2026 (Iran War), with 32+ confirmed incidents including 22+ vessel attacks, NO confirmed single oil spill exceeding 50,000 barrels has occurred. The most significant attack - on the fully laden VLCC Al-Salmi (~2M barrels) on March 31 - resulted in zero oil leakage per Dubai authorities. Modern double-hulled tankers have proven remarkably resilient, sustaining fires and structural damage without catastrophic hull breaches. Shipping traffic has dropped to near-zero, reducing targets. The Gulf of Oman specifically has seen only 3-4 of the 22+ attacks, all minor. While the conflict is ongoing and escalating (cruise missiles now in use), the 35-day empirical track record of zero large spills is powerful evidence. I estimate ~9% probability that a conflict-related spill exceeding 50,000 barrels will be reported in the Gulf of Oman before May 1, 2026.

factors.description: 35 days of active conflict with 22+ vessel attacks, yet ZERO confirmed large oil spills. Al-Salmi VLCC (fully laden ~2M barrels) confirmed NO oil leakage. Strongest empirical evidence.
Modern double-hulled tankers prove resilient to drone/missile attacks - fires and structural damage but no catastrophic hull breaches. No vessels sunk in 22+ attacks.
Shipping traffic through Strait of Hormuz dropped to near-zero, dramatically reducing laden tanker targets in the Gulf of Oman.
50,000 barrels threshold is very high. Even in 1980s Iran-Iraq tanker war (~400+ ships attacked), individual spills of this magnitude were rare.
Active and escalating conflict: Iran using cruise missiles, attacking anchored vessels in ports. US military campaign ongoing with possible further escalation over remaining 27 days.
Hundreds of laden tankers trapped/anchored in Persian Gulf region. 85+ loaded tankers with massive oil cargo present concentrated spill risk.
Question requires spill specifically in Gulf of Oman - only 3-4 of 22+ attacks occurred there, and those were minor. Most attacks concentrated in Persian Gulf/Strait of Hormuz.
Iran may have strategic incentive to avoid massive environmental damage to its own southern coastline.

reasoning:
**Forecast: 9% probability (logit: -2.3)**

**Key reasoning:**

Despite the most severe maritime conflict in decades — the 2026 Strait of Hormuz crisis with 32+ confirmed incidents and 22+ vessel attacks since Feb 28 — **no single large oil spill has been confirmed** anywhere in the region, let alone specifically in the Gulf of Oman.

The strongest evidence is empirical: the fully laden VLCC Al-Salmi (~2M barrels) was struck by an Iranian drone on March 31 and confirmed to have **zero oil leakage**. The QatarEnergy tanker Aqua 1 (hit by cruise missile April 1) also had **no environmental impact**. The only confirmed oil on water was from Sonangol Namibe in Iraqi waters — unquantified and likely small.

**Against YES:** Modern double-hulled tankers are remarkably resilient to the weapons used (drones, small missiles). None of 22+ attacked vessels has sunk. Traffic is near-zero, reducing targets. The Gulf of Oman specifically saw only 3-4 minor attacks. The 50,000 barrel threshold is very high.

**For YES:** The conflict is ongoing and escalating, with ~27 days remaining. Hundreds of laden tankers remain trapped. Iran is using increasingly powerful weapons. A single catastrophic breach of a laden VLCC could far exceed the threshold.

resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if ITOPF, UNEP, NOAA, or another credible source under the Metaculus policy at https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions reports before May 1, 2026 that a single spill larger than 50000 barrels occurred in the Gulf of Oman. Otherwise, this question resolves to no.
fine_print: The spill must be both conflict-related and larger than 50,000 barrels. Multiple smaller spills do not aggregate. Candidate Sources: ITOPF https://www.itopf.org/ ; UNEP https://www.unep.org/
